coredata.dc:title: Optimizing redox cycling with α-Fe2O3/MoS2 heterostructures for efficient degradation of antibiotics via peroxymonosulfate activation

coredata.dc:description: Iron-based materials are widely utilized in sulfate radical advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) due to their efficiency, cost-effectiveness, recyclability, and environmental sustainability. However, the continuous consumption of Fe(II) and the accumulation of excess Fe(III) often reduce catalytic efficiency and contribute to sludge formation. To overcome these limitations, we developed a novel α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite catalyst, synthesized via a hydrothermal process. The synergistic interaction between α-Fe2O3 and MoS2 enhances the redox cycling of Fe and Mo ions. This redox cycling accelerates PMS activation, promoting the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for tetracycline (TC) degradation. Compared with individual α-Fe2O3/PMS and MoS2/PMS systems, α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system exhibits preferable catalytic performance, achieving 92% TC removal rate within 30 min. Mechanistic investigations using radical quenching and electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments confirmed the involvement of both radical and non-radical pathways in the degradation process. The enhanced catalytic activity is attributed to the efficient adsorption and decomposition of PMS, facilitated by the optimized composite structure. Furthermore, microbial toxicology tests displayed the reduced toxicity of the degradation byproducts. This study underscores the potential of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite as a highly efficient catalyst in the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.

head.ce:title: Optimizing redox cycling with α-Fe2O3/MoS2 heterostructures for efficient degradation of antibiotics via peroxymonosulfate activation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron-based materials are widely utilized in sulfate radical advanced oxidation processes (SR-AOPs) due to their efficiency, cost-effectiveness, recyclability, and environmental sustainability. However, the continuous consumption of Fe(II) and the accumulation of excess Fe(III) often reduce catalytic efficiency and contribute to sludge formation. To overcome these limitations, we developed a novel α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite catalyst, synthesized via a hydrothermal process. The synergistic interaction between α-Fe2O3 and MoS2 enhances the redox cycling of Fe and Mo ions. This redox cycling accelerates PMS activation, promoting the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) for tetracycline (TC) degradation. Compared with individual α-Fe2O3/PMS and MoS2/PMS systems, α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system exhibits preferable catalytic performance, achieving 92% TC removal rate within 30 min. Mechanistic investigations using radical quenching and electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments confirmed the involvement of both radical and non-radical pathways in the degradation process. The enhanced catalytic activity is attributed to the efficient adsorption and decomposition of PMS, facilitated by the optimized composite structure. Furthermore, microbial toxicology tests displayed the reduced toxicity of the degradation byproducts. This study underscores the potential of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite as a highly efficient catalyst in the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the persistence of emerging contaminants, notably pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), have emerged as a significant environmental concern [1–6]. Of particular concern is antibiotic wastewater, typified by tetracycline (TC), which poses challenges to conventional biological treatment processes due to its inhibitory effects on microorganisms [7–9]. TC is extensively utilized globally owing to its efficacy, affordability, and broad spectrum. However, its poor absorption by organisms and resistance to removal in conventional sewage treatment facilities result in its accumulation in surface water bodies and eventual entry into the food chain. This continual rise in antibiotic levels in the environment poses serious threats to soil, water ecosystems, and human health [10]. A large number of studies indicate widespread occurrence of TC in natural soil and water environments, highlighting the urgency of addressing this environmental issue [11]. Traditional approaches to mitigating antibiotic pollution exhibit shortcomings such as incomplete purification, potential for secondary pollution, and being resource-intensive. Consequently, it is urgent to develop green and efficient methods for addressing this challenge. Currently, several techniques are employed for antibiotic removal, including adsorption method, advanced oxidation processes, membrane separation, and photocatalysis [12,13]. Among which, the advanced oxidation technique adopting persulfate activation (SR-AOP) stands out as a promising and reliable strategy.

Currently, the widely used persulfate compounds in water treatment are peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and persulfate (PDS) [14]. These compounds display sufficient stability and robust oxidation capabilities, operating effectively over a broad pH range. Additionally, persulfate is economically advantageous, being solid at room temperature, thus facilitating ease of transportation and storage. In comparison to PDS, PMS features asymmetric -S-O-O-H bonds within its HSO5 − structure, with the bond energy of -O-O- bond estimated to fall within the range of 140–213.3 kJ/mol. Upon exposure to additional energy or catalytic agents, these bonds readily cleave, activating the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as SO4 •−, SO5
−, O2
−, OH, and 1O2, thereby facilitating enhanced pollutant oxidation reactions. Conversely, the symmetric -S-O-O-S- bond in the structure of PDS limits its activation capability, wherein the -O-O- bond possesses a bond energy of 140 kJ/mol. While PDS can also be activated by external energy or catalysts to produce ROS, it undergoes decomposition solely as an electron acceptor to yield SO4
− [15–17]. Hence, numerous studies have demonstrated that PMS, owing to its asymmetric structure and higher bond energy, exhibits greater susceptibility to activation by metal oxide catalysts, thereby promoting the formation of SO4
− radicals [18,19]. Moreover, the activator of PMS boast relatively prolonged lifespans, with certain metal ions capable of regeneration through the redox cycle of PMS.

The inherent characteristics of iron (Fe), such as its non-toxic, safe, and abundant nature in the earth's crust, have rendered iron-based materials indispensable in catalysis. Iron oxides exist naturally in various forms, with magnetite (Fe3O4), magnehematite (γ-Fe2O3), and hematite (α-Fe2O3) being among the most prevalent. All of which are known to facilitate PMS activation [20]. As a n-type semiconductor, α-Fe2O3 has a suitable band gap and is widely used in electrocatalysis, photocatalysis and other fields. It not only displays high catalytic activity in the degradation of organic pollutants, but also shows great potential in energy conversion, such as water splitting to generate hydrogen, CO2 reduction, and N2 fixation. Especially in the activation process of PMS, α-Fe2O3 effectively promotes the decomposition of PMS by providing active sites, so as to degrade various of contaminants effectively [21–24]. However, the activation of PMS by iron-based materials presents challenges, notably in the reduction of high-valent transition metal ions. The conversion of Fe(III) on the iron oxide surface to Fe(II) via electron transfer reaction with PMS is essential for the generation of SO4
−. While the complexation of PMS on Fe(III) surfaces can boost electron transfer, enabling Fe(III) reduction by PMS, the incapability of Fe(III) to react with PMS to generate SO4
−, along with the unfavorable redox potential of Fe(III)/Fe(II), collectively imply a gradual attenuation in the catalytic activity of iron-based materials for activating PMS over successive reaction cycles [25,26]. Enhancing the cycle efficiency of Fe(III)/Fe(II) is paramount for improving the catalytic performance of iron-based catalysts. This will not only improves the stability but also enhances oxidation efficiency, thereby effectively reducing reagent costs [27]. In comparison to monometallic iron-based catalysts, bimetallic catalysts exhibit preferable stability, mitigating metal ion leaching, while simultaneously offering heightened redox activity and catalytic efficacy [28].

It is noteworthy that the reducibility of MoS2 renders it a valuable auxiliary catalyst for enhancing Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion [29]. Previous research has indicated that the addition of MoS2 to the Fe(II)/PMS system for sulfamethoxazole degradation not only improves reaction efficiency but also accelerates the rate-limiting step of Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion. This results in facilitating the oxidation of Mo(IV) to Mo(VI) within the reaction system [30]. Beyond expediting the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle in Fenton and Fenton-like processes, MoS2 can directly activate PMS to yield sulfate. It has been reported that in the MoS2/PMS system, Mo(IV) undergoes rapid oxidation to higher states (Mo(V) and Mo(VI)), breaking the oxygen-oxygen bond of PMS and generating a significant amount of freely diffused SO4
− [31]. Hence, the synergistic combination of iron-based materials and MoS2 serves dual purposes, it facilitates the activation of PMS by both Fe and MoS2 and enhances the promotion of the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycle by MoS2. This combined catalytic system enables efficient and rapid pollutant degradation without the generation of iron-containing sludge.

To address the challenges associated with the limited number of reaction sites and insufficient Fe(III)/Fe(II) conversion rates observed in iron-based activated persulfate (PMS) systems, a nanocomposite catalyst featuring α-Fe2O3/MoS2 heterogeneous structure was synthesized via hydrothermal method. This catalyst was developed as a PMS activator based on the modification approach of iron-based bimetallic catalytic systems, aiming to construct FeMo bimetallic activation system to enhance Fe(II)/Fe(III) redox cycling. Various techniques were employed to characterize the obtained catalysts, including X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. Comprehensive assessment of catalytic activities was conducted, encompassing reaction kinetics, influential factors, and stability. In order to delve deeper into the reaction mechanism, the role of reactive oxygen species was studied by adding different free radical quenchers and utilizing electron spin resonance (ESR) method. In conclusion, this work presents a viable approach for advancing sulfate radical-based advanced oxidation process technology towards sustainable, efficient, and low-energy development.
The reagents and instruments employed in this research are listed in the Supplementary information (Tables S1 and S2).

The synthesis of MoS2 nanoparticles was carried out using the following procedure: 1.07 g of ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O) were accurately weighed and dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water. The resulting mixture was homogenized via ultrasonic processing to ensure uniform dispersion. Subsequently, 0.77 g of thioacetamide were added into the solution, and the resulting suspension was subjected to magnetic stirring for 1 h. The reaction mixture was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 24 h. After that, the reactor was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The resultant black solid was separated from the reaction mixture by centrifugation, and subsequently washed multiple times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol to remove any impurities. The purified black solid was then dried at 60 °C for further characterization and analysis.

α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles were prepared as follows: 8.08 g Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was added into 100 mL of deionized water, ultrasonically dispersed homogeneously, and then stirred in a water bath at 60 °C for 1·h. Subsequently, 8 mol/L NaOH was added into the solution until the pH reached above 10 or more. The stirring procedure was kept at 60 °C for another 1 h. Followed by cooling, centrifugation, washing with water and alcohol, and drying. The dried solid was grind and then calcined in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 3 h with a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite was prepared as follows: 1.07 g ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O) and 0.20 g α-Fe2O3 powder were uniformly dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water, ultrasonically dispersed homogeneously, followed by the addition of 0.77 g thioacetamide, and the mixture was stirred magnetically for 1 h. The resulting solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled, washed with water and alcohol, dried, and then stored for further use. The detailed procedure is illustrated in Fig. 1a.

In this investigation, TC was chosen as the specific contaminant and degradation experiments were carried out in a glass reactor. 15 mg of α-Fe2O3, MoS2, α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite were added into TC solution (100 mL, 20 mg·L−1), respectively. After allowing for adsorption for 30 min, the solution was collected. Subsequently, 20 mg of PMS was added, and samples were collected at 2, 5, 10, 20, and 30 min during the reaction period. The samples were then subjected to high-speed centrifugation, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 357 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450) to determine the absorbance level. Further details regarding the experimental protocol for investigating the removal of TC under different influencing factors can be found in the Supplementary information (Text S1).

Four cycle experiments were conducted to assess the recyclability of the nanocomposite catalyst. Following each cycle, the solid phase catalyst underwent centrifugation to separate it, followed by washing with distilled water and ethanol to eliminate any residual TC or other reactive intermediates on the catalyst surface.

To further investigate the species of reactive oxygen species in α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system, 0.1 mmol of methanol (MeOH), p-benzoquinone (BQ), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and L-histidine (L-His) were added into 100 mL TC solution (20 mg·L−1) containing 15 mg of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. After adsorption for 30 min, 20 mg of PMS was added, and samples were collected at a reaction time of 2, 5, 10, 20 and 30 min. The samples were then subjected to high-speed centrifugation, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at a wavelength of 357 nm. Meanwhile, electron spin resonance (ESR) experiments were performed to further analyze the reactive oxygen species present in the system. The corresponding trapping agents were 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-n-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO), respectively.

The crystal structure of the obtained catalysts was analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Cu-Ka source with scanning angle from 10° to 80° at 7°/min. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) were used to observe the microstructure of the catalysts. The BET specific surface area and pore size distribution of the acquired samples were determined through N2 adsorption and desorption measurements conducted with a specific surface area analyzer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were utilized to identify the surface functional groups and elemental composition of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. Additionally, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a solution of 0.1 M KCl with 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−.
The details of this section are provided in the Supplementary information (Text S2).
The details of this section are provided in the Supplementary information (Text S3).

Prior to delving into the properties of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, it is imperative to validate its successful synthesis and elucidate its physicochemical attributes. The crystalline nature of α-Fe2O3, MoS2, and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite was explored via XRD measurement, as depicted in Fig. 1b. The diffraction peaks observed at 2θ = 24.14°, 33.15°, 35.61°, 40.85°, 49.48°, 54.09°, 62.45°, and 63.99° correspond to the (012), (104), (110), (113), (024), (116), (214), and (300) planes of α-Fe2O3, respectively (JCPDS NO. 33-0664) [32]. Similarly, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.39°, 32.68°, 33.51°, and 60.14° are attributed to the (002), (100), (101), and (008) crystal facets of MoS2, as indicated by JCPDS NO. 37-1492 [33]. Nevertheless, it is noteworthy that the XRD pattern of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite reveals no discernible characteristic peaks of α-Fe2O3, which may be attributed to the low introduction amount of α-Fe2O3 during the synthesis of the nanocomposite catalyst or the potential weakening of interlayer forces between MoS2 and α-Fe2O3 upon their combination. Similar observations have been documented in prior literature [34].

FT-IR spectra was presented in Fig. 1c. The absorption peak at 3468 cm−1 and the accompanying absorption at 1669 cm−1 denote the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups and the bending vibration of water molecules, respectively. Two distinct peaks at 468 and 551 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of FeO bond, confirming the presence of α-Fe2O3. Notably, the diffraction peaks observed in both α-Fe2O3 and MoS2 spectra are basically retained in α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. Upon interaction with MoS2, the characteristic peak of MoS at 485 cm−1 overlaps with the FeO peak, resulting in a broadened peak centered at 621 cm−1. Additionally, minor peaks at 1075 cm−1 and 1426 cm−1 are indicative of active site binding to sulfur complexes within MoS2 [35].

SEM and TEM measurements were employed to investigate the morphology and structural features of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. EDS analysis results confirm that Fe, O, Mo and S elements are successfully introduced into the prepared samples, and further indicated that α-Fe2O3 and MoS2 successfully recombined. The uniform distribution of these elements in the composite material can be observed through the distribution diagram, indicating that the composition of the sample displays high uniformity (Fig. 1d). Fig. 1e presents the microstructure of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, revealing nearly spherical nanoparticles within the sample. Furthermore, HRTEM images in Fig. 1f–g distinctly illustrates the lattice structure of the catalyst. The measured lattice spacings of 0.27 nm and 0.62 nm corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystallographic planes of MoS2, respectively. In addition, the spacings of 0.25 nm and 0.36 nm corresponding to the (110) and (012) planes of α-Fe2O3, which is consistent with the XRD analysis [33]. Furthermore, Fig. S1 displays the transformation of flower-like MoS2 into a uniform agglomerative structure via in-situ synthesis procedure. In addition, the structural characteristics of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 composites were determined by Raman spectrum analysis (Fig. S2). In summary, the formation of heterostructure between MoS2 and α-Fe2O3 is further elucidated.

To analyze the surface chemical composition and oxidation state of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, XPS measurement was conducted. The wide scan of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite is depicted in Fig. 2a, illustrating peaks corresponding to Mo 3d, S 2p, O 1s, and Fe 2p orbitals, affirming the presence of Mo, S, Fe, and O elements on the surface of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. Notably, a peak in the C 1s orbit is discernible, potentially originating from carbonaceous species in the ambient atmosphere. The C 1s curve is delineated into two peaks, as depicted in Fig. 2b, where the 288.2 eV peak corresponds to the CO bond, while the 284.8 eV peak is assigned to the CC bond [36–39]. After the incorporation of α-Fe2O3 into MoS2, the obtained O 1s spectrum exhibits three distinct peaks located at 530.78 eV, 531.86 eV, and 532.76 eV, corresponding to FeO, adsorbed oxygen (Oads), and hydroxyl (Ow), respectively (Fig. 2c) [33]. In the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d), the presence of four peaks at 161.77 eV, 163.09 eV, 168.79 eV, and 170.03 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 states in metal sulfides and metal sulfates [40,41]. The XPS spectrum of Mo (Fig. 2e) unveils the appearance of ≡Mo(V) (232.55 eV) and ≡Mo(VI) (235.82 eV) on the MoS2 surface, subsequent to its recombination with α-Fe2O3. The emergence of high-valence Mo species suggests an oxidation-reduction reaction upon α-Fe2O3 and MoS2 interaction. Furthermore, Fig. 2f illustrates the detailed spectrum of Fe 2p, which can be segregated into five main peaks. The peaks observed at 718.09 eV and 731.75 eV are identified as satellite peaks, while the peak situated at 710.06 eV is associated with Fe(II) 2p3/2. The peaks at 712.30 eV and 724.14 eV correspond to Fe(III) 2p3/2 and Fe(III) 2p1/2, respectively [42,43]. It is important to note that the overall Fe 2p XPS peak position for α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite shifts towards a higher binding energy. Furthermore, the XPS peak of O 1s exhibits a similar trend, moving towards a higher binding energy as well [34]. After the interaction with MoS2, the relative abundance of ≡Fe(II) on the α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite surface increases, attributable to the lower electronegativity of Fe compared to Mo, thereby triggering a redox cycle [44]. This characterization further corroborates the successful preparation of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, highlighting the robust interfacial bonding between the constituent materials [45,46].

The data for N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution are presented in Fig. 2g–i. The specific surface area and pore size characteristics of the catalyst play pivotal roles in determining its catalytic efficiency. The specific surface area and total pore volume of α-Fe2O3 (84.4819 m2/g, 0.5027 cm3/g) markedly exceed those of MoS2 (6.4713 m2/g, 0.0300 cm3/g). Nevertheless, upon integration into α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, these metrics decrease to 9.5807 m2/g and 0.0356 cm3/g, respectively (Table S3). While the specific surface area of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite slightly increases compared to MoS2, it remains significantly lower than that of α-Fe2O3. This is likely due to the introduction of α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles, which may increase adsorption sites. However, the increase is less pronounced than that observed with α-Fe2O3 alone. This suggests that α-Fe2O3 nanoparticles may partially occupy MoS2 adsorption sites upon integration, with insufficient loading compared to MoS2. In line with IUPAC classification, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite manifest characteristic features, notably exhibiting type IV isotherms and type III hysteresis loops at high relative pressures within the 0.4–1 range. Moreover, the analysis of pore size distribution diagram highlights that the pore size distribution of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite predominantly lies within the 0–50 nm range, indicative of mesoporous-rich surface structure. This multistage porous architecture enhances both adsorption capacity and abundance of catalytic active sites, thereby facilitating reactant mass transfer and ultimately enhancing catalytic reaction rates [47].

The presence of antibiotics poses a certain threat to the aquatic-environment and biological health. Meanwhile, the complexity of the real aquatic environment may influence the performance of catalysts. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the degradation of TC under different catalytic systems to evaluate the performance of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. In Fig. 3a, the degradation of TC via different catalytic systems, including PMS autoxidation, α-Fe2O3 combined with PMS, MoS2 combined with PMS, and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite combined with PMS, are examined. It is evident that PMS autoxidation alone can degrade TC, albeit with a removal rate of only 39.5% in 30 min and a slow degradation rate, insufficient for daily wastewater treatment requirements. The effect of MoS2 acting together with PMS was comparable to that of PMS alone, with a removal rate of only 41.6% in 30 min, indicating a degradation rate like PMS autoxidation, significantly lower than that of the nanocomposite catalyst. It is noteworthy that the removal rate of α-Fe2O3/PMS system can reach 67.7% in 30 min, and the degradation rate is significantly higher. In contrast, α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system achieves a remarkable TC removal rate of 92% within 30 min, accompanied by a significantly enhanced degradation rate compared to the α-Fe2O3/PMS system. For α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system, the corresponding TOC removal rate is 49% within 30 min (Fig. S4). Fig. 3b illustrates the use of pseudo-first-order kinetic model to simulate the degradation plots of TC, facilitating a more effective comparison of the reaction rates. It is evident that α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system exhibits the highest rate constant for TC at 0.0928 min−1 (R2 = 0.925), surpassing that of α-Fe2O3/PMS system (0.0422 min−1, R2 = 0.952) and MoS2/PMS system (0.0197 min−1, R2 = 0.951) by 2.2 and 4.7 times, respectively. This significant enhancement further underscores the synergistic effect of α-Fe2O3-modified MoS2 nanocomposite catalyst, which accelerates the cycling of Fe ions, promotes PMS activation, and expedites TC degradation [48]. In conclusion, the outstanding degradation ability of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite serves as a basis for its application in treating antibiotic-contaminated wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of reaction parameters on the degradation of TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to optimize the reaction conditions, a systematic assessment of the impacts of different factors on the degradation process was conducted. The effects of different catalyst concentration, PMS concentration, initial pH values, TC concentration, and co-existing ions on the degradation of TC in α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system were investigated, as depicted in Fig. 3c–l. In Fig. 3c–d, increasing the catalyst concentration result in faster TC degradation (The k value increases from 0.0532 min−1 to 0.0900 min−1), which is attributed to the increased activation of PMS as the number of catalytic sites on the catalyst improved with the catalyst content. Similarly, when the PMS concentration is increased from 0.1 g/L to 0.2 g/L (The k value increases from 0.0660 min−1 to 0.0898 min−1), the removal rate of TC increases by nearly 10% (Fig. 3e–f). This increasement is attributed to the higher PMS content facilitating its interaction with the catalyst, thereby generating more active oxygen species. When the concentration of catalyst and PMS is 0.15 g/L and 0.2 g/L, respectively, TC can be removed to the maximum extent, with the rate constant (k value) showing minimal change with increasing dosage of these parameters. Consequently, for cost-effectiveness, subsequent experiments adopt 0.15 g/L of catalyst and 0.2 g/L of PMS. Fig. 3g illustrates the effects of different TC concentrations on degradation efficiency. As the initial TC concentration increases to 40 mg/L, the degradation rate decreases to 75% after 30 min, accompanied by a reduction in the k value to 0.0583 min−1 (Fig. 3h). This may be attributed to the insufficient amount of reactive oxygen species in the system resulting in the inability to completely degrade the high concentration of TC. Conversely, at a relatively low TC concentration (10 mg/L), α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system efficiently enriches TC molecules, achieving a removal rate of up to 99%. The efficient and stable degradation rate at TC concentrations of 10–40 mg/L further demonstrate the preferable catalytic performance of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite.

It is widely acknowledged that the actual antibiotic wastewater contains other contaminants, potentially affecting wastewater pH. The initial solution pH is pivotal in PMS activation systems, necessitating an exploration of its impact on the entire reaction system. To this end, a series of experiments spanning a pH range of 3–11 were conducted to investigate its effect on TC degradation, as illustrated in Fig. 3i–j. Remarkably, α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite maintains close to 90% TC degradation efficiency over a wide range of pH values (3–9), with the best catalytic performance appeared in the pristine TC solution with unadjusted pH. When the initial pH is adjusted to be strongly alkaline (pH = 11), the TC removal rate only experience a slight decrease, yet still reaching 80 %. The phenomenon of decreased removal rate may be due to the presence of free radicals in the form of SO4
− (pH < 9), SO4
− and OH coexistence (pH 9–11), and OH (pH > l1), respectively [49]. Under highly alkaline conditions (pH = 11), a substantial amount of OH is generated (SO4
− + OH− → OH + SO4 2−), exerting dominance over the oxidative degradation process. The half-life and oxidation potential of OH are widely recognized to be significantly lower than those of SO4
−, affirming a considerably reduced oxidizing ability of OH compared to SO4
− [50]. Consequently, under OH domination, a reasonable reduction in TC removal rate occurs. Nevertheless, the overall removal efficiencies of α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system for TC consistently exceed 80% across the pH range of 3–11, emphasizing the practical applicability of the system. This observation also highlights the promising prospects for development of α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system in the field of practical wastewater treatment.

Inorganic ions and natural organic matter are commonly found in real water bodies and may interfere with TC degradation. To investigate the hindrance caused by the large amount of ionic states in environmental wastewater on degrade TC, excess anions (Cl−, NO3 −, H2PO4 −and SO4 2−) were added into α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system to investigate the possible effects. As shown in Fig. 3k–l, Cl−, NO3 − and SO4 2− interfere minimal interference with TC removal, with negligible effects on degradation rates (The degradation rates of TC were 88%, 91%, and 88%, and the corresponding k values were 0.0789 min−1, 0.0826 min−1 and 0.0791 min−1, respectively). In contrast, H2PO4 − significantly inhibits the removal process of TC, achieving a removal rate of only 29% and an overall slow degradation rate (k = 0.0149 min−1). This phenomenon can be explained by the reaction of H2PO4 − with Fe3+ on the catalyst to generate Fe-p complexes such as Fe(H2O)4(H2PO4)2+ and Fe(H2O)4(H2PO4)2 +, which hinder the Fe(III)/Fe(II) cycling and lead to the decrease of catalytic activity [51]. Overall, the effect of inorganic ions on TC removal is generally insignificant except for H2PO4 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability, stability and eco-toxicity of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The potential for practical applications of catalysts hinges not just on their effectiveness in target pollutants, but also on their stability and ability to be reused. Therefore, five cycling experiments were conducted under the same reaction conditions to investigate the reusability of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, with the removal efficiencies depicted in Fig. 4a. The degradation efficiencies of the four cycling experiments were 92%, 67%, 56%, and 48%, respectively. The noticeable decrease in TC removal can be attributed to: (1) the elution of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite may result in reduced reusability when compared to the fresh catalysts, (2) the agglomeration of intermediates generated during the reaction process around the surface of the catalysts resulted in a reduction of active sites. This will impeded the reusability of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite interaction with PMS, leading to a decrease in catalytic performance [52]. However, it has been observed that ultrasonic treatment unexpectedly restores the catalytic activity of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. This phenomenon may be attributed to the intense mechanical forces generated by high-speed shock waves during ultrasonic exposure. These forces effectively remove surface and pore blockages on α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, thereby recovering its specific surface area and pore volume. As a result, the catalytic performance of the nanocomposite catalyst is reinstated [53,54]. In conclusion, α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite displays certain reusability.

To achieve a more profound understanding of the variations in phase composition of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite before and after TC degradation, a comprehensive analysis of XRD spectra was conducted. Fig. 4b illustrates that the XRD spectra obtained from the catalyst, both pre- and post-reaction, demonstrate no notable changes, underscoring the resilience and longevity of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite. This stability may stem from the robust interactions between α-Fe2O3 and MoS2. Furthermore, XPS spectra from the utilized sample are also depicted (Figs. 4c and S3). It is observed that the distinctive diffraction peaks, both before and after the reaction, remain highly aligned with those of the original catalyst, with no significant shifts in peak positions. These findings corroborate the XRD results, which indicate that the catalyst possesses commendable stability. In addition, as a magnetic catalyst, α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite exhibits enhanced performance during the recycling process (Fig. S5), further indicating its potential for application in the treatment of antibiotic wastewater.

Furthermore, an ecotoxicity assessment utilizing E. coli was performed to determine the toxicity of the byproducts generated from the degradation of TC over α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system (Fig. 4d). The antimicrobial activity of α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS degraded TC solution was effectively reduced by comparing with the blank experiment. This outcome corroborates the system's capability to mitigate the toxicity of antibiotics and effectively prevent secondary contamination, underscoring α-Fe2O3/MoS2 as an environment ally friendly composite material.

PMS activation has been documented to engage in two distinct pathways: free radical (SO4
−, O2
−, OH) and non-free radical (1O2 and mediated electron transfer). To investigate the regulatory mechanism of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system, various quenching agents were employed to discern the ROS species involved in the degradation process. MeOH was chosen as the quenching agent for OH (with a rate constant of k = 9.7 × 108 M−1·s−1) and SO4
− (with a rate constant of k = 2.5 × 107 M−1·s−1), while BQ was utilized as the quenching agent for O2
−, and DMSO was employed as the quenching agent for OH. Additionally, L-His was introduced as a scavenger for 1O2. As illustrated in Figs. 5a–b and S6, the application of different scavengers resulted in inhibitory effects on the degradation of TC, indicating the involvement of OH, SO4
−, O2
−, and 1O2 in the heterogeneous reaction. Upon the addition of MeOH, the removal rate of TC in α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system decreases to 59.9% (k = 0.03964 min−1). Furthermore, the decrease in TC degradation rate to around 33.5% (k = 0.01884 min−1) upon the addition of DMSO, implying that SO4
− in the liquid phase may not be the primary ROS species involved. Concurrently, with the introduction of BQ and L-His, the degradation efficiency diminish significantly, resulting in removal rates of only 62.1% and 52.2% (k = 0.03495 min−1 and 0.03424 min−1), respectively. This observation underscores the significant roles played by O2
− and 1O2 in the reaction process [55].

To delve deeper into the reactive oxygen species generated during TC degradation within α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrrolin-n-oxide (DMPO) was employed as the trapping agent for OH, SO4
−, and O2
−, while 2,2,6,6-tetroxypiperidine oxide (TEMP) served as the trapping agent for 1O2. Detection was conducted employing the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the ESR spectrum displays the DMPO-SO4
− signal (with a relative intensity of 1:1:1:1:1:1) and the DMPO-OH signal (with a relative intensity of 1:2:2:1). Consistent with findings from previous PMS-based oxidation systems, the signal intensity of OH is notably higher than that of SO4
− free radicals, aligning with outcomes observed in quenching experiments [32,44]. Within this system, the characteristic peaks of O2
− captured by DMPO are illustrated in Fig. 5d, manifesting four distinct peaks, indicating the involvement of O2
− in the degradation reaction. Moreover, O2
− not only reacts with OH to generate 1O2 but also contributes to 1O2 formation through self-recombination [56]. Furthermore, the triple signal in a 1:1:1 ratio shown in Fig. 5e aligns with the distinct peak corresponding to the TEMP-1O2 adduct [47]. Importantly, these signals demonstrate a gradual increase over time, indicating the continuous generation of OH, SO4
−, O2
−, and 1O2, which are all crucial for the degradation of TC.

Furthermore, to substantiate the existence of electron transfer pathway, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was utilized to analyze the charge transfer properties of the catalysts [57]. The EIS curves for MoS2, α-Fe2O3, and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 5f. Clearly, the Nyquist plot for α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite shows the least semicircular diameter, indicating that the incorporation of α-Fe2O3 into MoS2 promotes electron transfer to a certain extent. This observation also suggests the presence of electron transfer pathways within α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system. In summation, both pathways, including free radical and non-free radical mechanisms, play important roles in the breakdown of TC within the α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system. The proposed pathways for TC degradation is displayed in Fig. S7.

To further evaluate the deep-level impact of MoS2 incorporation on the atomic-scale structure of α-Fe2O3, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted based on the obtained structural and compositional information for α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, aiming to understand the intrinsic mechanism other than the enhanced catalytic performance. From the geometric optimization results (Fig. 6a), α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite exhibits a clear layered stacking structure, indicating that the introduction of MoS2 enhances the structural stability of α-Fe2O3, and provides more active sites, which contribute to the enhanced catalytic activity. The optimized structure of the nanocomposite displays favorable stability during adsorption and reaction processes, particularly the heterojunction interface facilitating rapid electron transfer, effectively lowering the reaction energy barrier [58]. Adsorption energy calculations show that α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite displays stronger PMS adsorption, with an adsorption energy of −3.425 eV, which is significantly higher than that of α-Fe2O3 (−3.334 eV). Additionally, the slight elongation of OO bond suggests that the nanocomposite favors the activation of oxygen molecules, promoting the generation of reactive oxygen species during reactions (Fig. 6b). The differential charge density mainly reflect the redistribution of electrons during adsorption (Fig. 6c). The diagrams of α-Fe2O3 and α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite show the gain and loss of electrons at the adsorption interface (yellow regions represent electron accumulation, while blue regions indicate electron depletion) [59,60]. Clearly, the introduction of MoS2 significantly alters the electron distribution at the interface, making charge transfer more pronounced. The strong electronic coupling between MoS2 and α-Fe2O3 helps accelerate the transportation of electrons, thereby enhancing catalytic activity. Overall, α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite shows great potential in PMS activation via enhancing electron transfer and adsorption capabilities.

Consequently, the catalytic mechanism of α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS within this heterogeneous system is illustrated in Fig. 7 . Acting as an electron donor, MoS2 provides electrons to ≡Fe(III), successfully reducing it to ≡Fe(II) (Eq. (1)). Given that Fe(III) possesses higher standard redox potentials (E0 (Fe(III)/Fe(II)) = 0.77 V vs. NHE) compared to ≡Mo(VI) (E0 (Mo(VI)/Mo(IV)) = 0.65 V vs. NHE), MoS2 facilitates the acceleration of the rate-limiting step of ≡Fe(III)/≡Fe(II) by exposing ≡Mo(IV) active sites (Eq. (2)) [61]. This exposure boosts the reduction of ≡Fe(III) to ≡Fe(II) and activates PMS by uninterruptedly generating ≡Fe(II). Additionally, Fe(III) present on the α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite surface reacts with HSO5 − to acquire electrons, subsequently converting to ≡Fe(II). These ≡Fe(II) species further react with HSO5 − to yield SO4
− and OH, thus establishing a redox cycle between ≡Fe(III) and ≡Fe(II) that continuously generates SO4
− and OH (Eqs. (3)–(5)) [16,62,63]. Remarkably, the exposed ≡Mo(IV) species in MoS2 can also activate PMS, reacting with HSO5 − to produce SO4
−, as depicted in Eqs. (6), (7). Furthermore, O2
− within the system can be generated through various reactions (Eqs. (8), (9)) [64]. The relatively long lifespan of 1O2 (2–3.5 μs) enables its diffusion into the solution for oxidation, making it a crucial ROS in the system [65]. The reduction of ≡Mo(VI) can facilitate the formation of 1O2 (Eq. (10)) [66]. Moreover, SO5
−, produced after HSO5 − reacts with transition metal ions and loses an electron in the reaction solution, can react with H2O molecules to generate 1O2 (Eq. (11)) [67,68]. Additionally, in certain instances, PMS itself can activate the formation of 1O2 (Eq. (12)) [69,70]. Ultimately, the TC molecules undergo assault by the ROS generated during the process, resulting in their degradation into smaller molecular intermediates, which are subsequently transformed into CO2 and H2O (Eq. (13)). In summary, the potential mechanisms underlying α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system include: (a) Activation of PMS by ≡Fe(II) species residing on the surface of α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, leading to the generation of free radicals; (b) Activation of PMS by ≡Mo(IV) species present on the α-Fe2O3/MoS2 surface, resulting in the formation of reactive intermediates; (c) Utilization of ≡Mo(IV) as an electron donor to expedite the redox cycle of ≡Fe(III)/≡Fe(II), thereby enhancing the catalytic activity and stability of the catalyst.

Antibiotic pollution in wastewater presents a significant ecological and human health hazard. This study focuses on addressing this issue by synthesizing α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite via a hydrothermal method, which serves as an efficient non-homogeneous catalyst for activating PMS to remove TC from wastewater. Notably, α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system demonstrates superior catalytic efficiency in TC degradation compared to other catalytic systems. This heightened catalytic performance may be attributed to the synergistic effects between α-Fe2O3 and MoS2, which expedite the redox cycling of Fe and Mo ions, thereby facilitating PMS adsorption and decomposition. Consequently, this process generates a greater quantity of active free radicals, enhancing TC degradation. The inclusion of α-Fe2O3 improves the pH adaptability, enabling α-Fe2O3/MoS2 to activate PMS across a wide pH range (pH 3–11) for TC degradation, thereby augmenting its practical utility. Furthermore, α-Fe2O3/MoS2 nanocomposite, serving as a magnetic catalyst, exhibiting enhanced recyclability, thereby reducing operational costs. The evaluation of toxicity indicates that the system is capable of decreasing the harmful effects of pollutants and preventing secondary contamination. In general, α-Fe2O3/MoS2/PMS system offers an efficient and environmentally friendly approach for TC removal from wastewater, highlighting its potential as a promising candidate for practical applications in wastewater treatment technologies.
